text: Full length article Mass transport deposits in confined environment, a case study from the Kaiping sag, Pearl River Mouth basin, northern south China sea margin Lushan Jiang , Xiwu Luan * College of Earth Science and Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, Shandong 266590, China A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Mass transport deposits Confined environment Compression High sedimentation rate Sea level change The Kaiping sag A B S T R A C T Mass transport deposits (MTDs) have been extensively discussed in the literature. The prevailing understanding characterizes MTDs as gravity-driven instability phenomena occurring on the seafloors. This study employs high- resolution 2D/3D seismic data integrated with well data to re-evaluate the genesis mechanisms of MTDs in the Kaiping sag, Zhu-II depression, Pearl River Mouth basin (PRMB), northern margin of the South China Sea (SCS). Key diagnostic features interpreted from seismic datasets reveal the absence of typical characteristics of con- ventional MTDs, including no distinct headwall scarps, and moats, and especially no onlapping structures of isochronous turbidite contourites that usually cover uneven MTD surfaces. In contrast, seismic evidence for a tectonic compression environment is observed, such as thrust fault reactivation, localized stratal folding, and chaotic thrust-related deformation patterns within the study interval-consistent with the post-spreading tectonic setting of the SCS. For the first time, this research concludes that these MTDs formed in a confined environment (rather than an open continental slope), and were induced by mega-earthquakes. The key contributing factors to their formation include: (1) tectonic compression after the cessation of SCS seafloor spreading (post-16 Ma), evidenced by seismic records of thrusting and folding; (2) elevated sedimentation rates since the peak uplift of the eastern Tibetan plateau (since 15 Ma), leading to thick unconsolidated sediments; (3) periods of higher sea level

the first time, this research concludes that these MTDs formed in a confined environment (rather than an open continental slope), and were induced by mega-earthquakes. The key contributing factors to their formation include: (1) tectonic compression after the cessation of SCS seafloor spreading (post-16 Ma), evidenced by seismic records of thrusting and folding; (2) elevated sedimentation rates since the peak uplift of the eastern Tibetan plateau (since 15 Ma), leading to thick unconsolidated sediments; (3) periods of higher sea level (favouring high pore-water pressure); and (4) mega-earthquakes triggered by the post-spreading compression. Collectively, these factors facilitated the liquefaction processes that drove MTDs development. 1. Introduction Mass transports, including slides, slumps, and debris flows, consti- tute critical gravity-driven sedimentary processes on continental slope, exhibiting well-documented variability in their movement mechanics and depositional characteristics (Murray and Renard, 1891; Varnes, 1958; Dott, 1963; Shanmugam, 2012, 2020; Palladino and Giordano, 2019; Alves, 2015; Deckers, 2015; Gee et al., 2007). Specifically, slides demonstrate consistent translational movement downslope with mini- mal internal deformation. Slumps, by contrast, involve rotational displacement along a concave-up surface, characterized by significant internal stratigraphic disruption. Debris flows, which originate from water-saturated slumps, behave as plastic laminar flows dominated by intergranular shear (Alves, 2015; Deckers, 2015; Gee et al., 2007). To date, nearly all globally reported MTDs- regardless of detection methods (e.g., side-scan sonar, multibeam echosounder, sub-bottom profiler), or morphological types (slide, slump, debris flow) −have been associated with open continental margin settings. In these environments, unconfined downslope movement is unimpeded by basin-bounding structures or overlying sediment loads (Lamarche et al., 2008; Strasser et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2013). The application of 3D seismic data has significantly improved our understanding of MTD geometry, spatial extent, and depositional genesis (Alves, 2010), while prompting re- visions to conventional MTD conceptual models (He et al.,

sonar, multibeam echosounder, sub-bottom profiler), or morphological types (slide, slump, debris flow) −have been associated with open continental margin settings. In these environments, unconfined downslope movement is unimpeded by basin-bounding structures or overlying sediment loads (Lamarche et al., 2008; Strasser et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2013). The application of 3D seismic data has significantly improved our understanding of MTD geometry, spatial extent, and depositional genesis (Alves, 2010), while prompting re- visions to conventional MTD conceptual models (He et al., 2013; Gong et al., 2014). Additionally, MTDs have gained substantial economic importance, as numerous deep-water petroleum reservoirs worldwide produce hydrocarbons from MTD-hosted sandstones (Shanmugam, 2012, 2020). Despite these advancements, a critical research gap re- mains: the formation mechanisms, diagnostic seismic features, and triggering factors of MTDs in confined environments (e.g., sedimentary soft layers bounded and buried by overlying strata) remain largely un- explored. Existing knowledge basically excludes such settings, thereby limiting our ability to fully understand formation processes and comprehensively characterize MTDs. Compression-induced uplift of Taiwan and Dongsha during this period is expected to generate mega-earthquakes in the sag region; (2) high sedimentation rates, driven by the peak uplift of the eastern Tibetan plateau (since 15 Ma), have deposited a thick unconsolidated stratum within the sag; and (3) periodic high sea-level stands have formed highly saturated argillaceous deposits in the sag, providing foundation for the development of MTDs (Dai et al., 1998; Pang et al., 1995). The pri- mary objectives of this study are: (1) to identify the diagnostic seismic features of MTDs in the Kaiping sag by integrating 2D/3D seismic and well data, and to compare these features with those of conventional open-environment MTDs; (2) to evaluate the roles of key controlling factors (tectonic compression, sedimentation rate, sea-level change, and mega-earthquakes) on MTD development; and (3) to extend the MTD conceptual model to include confined environments, thereby addressing an existing gap in MTD research. 2. Geological setting The SCS basin is floored primarily by oceanic crust at central and by rifted continental crusts at margins, and by continent-ocean boundary (COB) crust in between (Zhang et al., 2021a, 2024; Zhang and Luan, 2012) (Fig. 1). The oceanic SCS basin is complicated by extinct spreading ridges, transform faults, and seamounts (Yan et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2012). primarily by oceanic crust at central and by rifted continental crusts at margins, and by continent-ocean boundary (COB) crust in between (Zhang et al., 2021a, 2024; Zhang and Luan, 2012) (Fig. 1). The oceanic SCS basin is complicated by extinct spreading ridges, transform faults, and seamounts (Yan et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2012). The continental margins underwent rifting, sedimentation, and deformation in response to multiple tectonic processes: the collision, subduction of the Philippine plate to the Eurasian plate, the uplift associated with of the Himalayan orogeny (Ding et al., 2022), the spreading of the oceanic SCS basin itself, and subsequent post-spreading activities (Lu et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2014; Larsen et al., 2018). These processed collectively evolved the region into the present-day wide rifting system (Corti et al., 2003; England, 1983; Buck, 1991; Benes and Davy, 1996; Brun et al., 1994). Their various ages related to oceanic spreading, continental rifting and post spreading volcanic and magmatic activities are determined by the Ocean Discovery Program (ODP) Expedition 184 (Wang et al., 2000), and the International Ocean Dis- covery Program (IODP) Expedition 349, 367/368 (Expedition 349 Sci- entists, 2014; Expedition 367/368 Scientists, 2018; Larsen et al., 2018), and by industry wells at marginal basins (Fig. 1). The East Sub-basin started opening at ~ 30 Ma (Zhang et al., 2024) stopped around 15 Ma, and the Southwest Sub-basin initiated at ~ 23.6 Ma and terminated at about 16 Ma (Li et al., 2015). The Luzon arc is believed to be formed at about 15 Ma (Briais et al., 1993; Yang et al., 1996). The Taiwan orogeny started to buildup due to the westward movement of the Luzon arc and its oblique collision with the Eurasian continental margin (Teng, 1990). The Cenozoic magmatism in the region can be divided into three stages:

around 15 Ma, and the Southwest Sub-basin initiated at ~ 23.6 Ma and terminated at about 16 Ma (Li et al., 2015). The Luzon arc is believed to be formed at about 15 Ma (Briais et al., 1993; Yang et al., 1996). The Taiwan orogeny started to buildup due to the westward movement of the Luzon arc and its oblique collision with the Eurasian continental margin (Teng, 1990). The Cenozoic magmatism in the region can be divided into three stages: the Paleocene-Eocene pre-spreading stage (>33 Ma), the Oligocene- middle Miocene spreading stage (33–15 Ma), and the late Miocene- Quaternary post-spreading stage (<15 Ma) (Yan et al., 2006). The pre- spreading magmatism predominantly occurred on the northern margin of the SCS (Xu et al., 2012). The magmatism contemporaneous with seafloor spreading was very weak in the margins mainly occurring at the fault intersections within the extensional sags (Yan et al., 2006). The post-spreading magmatism is strong along the lower slope of the northern margin of the SCS and in the central basin (Lüdmann and Wong, 1999; Lüdmann et al., 2001; Yan et al., 2001). The most known major seamounts in the central SCS basin are believed to be emplaced after the seafloor spreading and all younger than 15 Ma (Wang et al., 1985; Kudrass et al., 1986; Yan et al., 2008; Expedition 349 Scientists, 2014). Most of these seamounts are along, or to the north of, the relict spreading center and are composed mainly of alkali basalts with sub- ordinate tholeiites that display oceanic island basalt-type (OIB-type) geochemical characteristics (Xu et al., 2012) (Fig. 1). The tectonic evolution of the PRMB can be divided into rifting, depression and compression stages (Clift and Lin, 2001; Pang et al., 1995, 2006). The rifting stage experienced the Shenhu movement, the first and the second episodes of the Zhuqiong movement lasting from the Late Cretaceous to the Early Oligocene (~53–30 Ma) (Liu et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2018). The depression stage experienced the Nanhai movement and the Baiyun movement lasting from the Middle Oligocene to the Early-Middle Miocene (30–10.5 Ma) coeval with the spreading of the SCS basin and the global sea-level rising (Shi et al., 2014). The compression stage experienced the Dongsha movement (since 10.5 Ma), causing thrust and compression deformation, accompanied by magmatic activity (Jiang et al., 2018) (Fig. 2). Generally, the sag (e.g. the Kaiping sag) of the PRMB has a Mesozoic igneous basement overlain by Cenozoic sedi- mentary unit (Luan, 2024). From bottom to top, these include the Eocene Wenchang and Enping Formations of mainly lacustrine mud- stones, the Oligocene Zhuhai Formation of mainly marine delta sand- stones, the Miocene Zhujiang, Hanjiang, and Yuehai Formations, the Pliocene Wanshan Formation, and the Quaternary deposits of mainly marine mudstones (Shi et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2024; Li, et al., 2025; Guan et al., 2016) (Fig. 2). As already revealed by the industry wells, the Wenchang Formation of semi-deep lacustrine mudstones and the Enping Formation of coal- bearing lacustrine mudstone are crucial hydrocarbon source rocks (Pang et al., 1995, 2006; Wang et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2022). The Zhuhai Formation (32–23.8 Ma) of giant neritic shelf delta deposits is charac- terized by southward-enhanced thermal subsidence along the southern slope of the Shenhu-Panyu low uplift (Liu et al., 2011). The Baiyun movement (23.8–10.5 Ma) was associated with the southward shift of the mid-ocean ridge in the SCS (Hall, 2002; Zhou et al., 2015). The movement cause rapid basement subsidence and sedimentary environ- ments shifting from shallow-water shelf (Zhuhai Formation) to deep- water (Zhujiang, Hanjiang and Yuehai Formations) on the PRMB (Pang et al., 2007; Wang et al., 2012) (Fig. 2). During the Upper Zhu- jiang Formation (21–16 Ma), marine transgression submerged the cen- tral uplift (the Panyu low uplift and the Shenhu uplift). A shallow water deltaic sedimentary system developed in the Upper Zhujiang Formation, characterized by multiple reservoir-seal assemblages comprising delta sands, and transgressive mudstones. The Dongsha movement was characterized by rejuvenated normal faults as preserved through the offsetting of the Wanshan Formation and the Quaternary-aged strata (Zhou et al., 2015). The Kaiping sag is located on the southwest of the Zhu-II depression (Figs. 1, 3). Lying in center of the Zhu-II depression of the PRMB, the NE- ENE trending Kaiping sag consists of a series of NE-ENE faulted half- grabens and grabens, with an area of about 6200 km2, and sediment thickness exceeding 5 km (Dai et al., 1998; Pang et al., 1995). The Cenozoic fills in the Kaiping sag can be subdivided into non-marine syn- rift and overlying marine post-rift mega-sequences separated by a basin- wide T70 unconformity (Dai et al., 1998) (Fig. 2). The Kaiping sag is tectonically bounded by uplifted blocks, creating a closed depositional system that restricts lateral sediment dispersal. The northern boundary: Shenhu uplift, a major structural high that blocks northward sediment transport and acts as a source of clastic material during uplift events (Guan et al., 2016); The southwestern boundary: Kaiping low uplift, a fault-bounded ridge that limits sediment escape to the southwest and controls the sag’s southern depocenter (Dai et al., 1998); The eastern boundary: Yunkai low uplift, which isolates the sag from the adjacent Baiyun sag and focuses sediment accumulation in the sag’s central area (Wang et al., 2018) (Figs. 1, 3). This boundary configuration traps sediments delivered by the Pearl River system and eroded from sur- rounding uplifts, preventing the downslope dispersal typical of open continental margins. This boundary configuration traps sediments delivered by the Pearl River system and eroded from sur- rounding uplifts, preventing the downslope dispersal typical of open continental margins. Instead, sediments accumulate vertically within the sag, building thick, continuous successions-essential for forming the “overlying strata load” that defines the confined MTD environment. The Kaiping sag experienced elevated sedimentation rates from the Middle Miocene onwards (~15 Ma), coinciding with the peak uplift of the eastern Tibetan plateau (Royden et al., 2008; Ding et al., 2022). This uplift enhanced sediment supply to the PRMB, with the Kaiping sag acting as a primary depocenter. High sedimentation rates prevented sufficient compaction and cementation of sediments, maintaining thick unconsolidated succession. Critically, this high porosity and high pore- water pressure succession facilitate fluidization when triggered by tec- tonic events, thus providing foundation for the confined MTD development. 3. Data and methodology 3.1. Seismic database Three − dimensional (3D) seismic data spanning approximately 2500 km2 within the Kaiping sag were employed (Fig. 3). The 3D seismic data were acquired using 8 streamers each of 6000 m long in a group interval of 12.5 m and in a cable distance equally of 100 m. The streamers were towed 7–9 m below the sea surface. Two 5085 in3-air- gun arrays were deployed 6 m beneath the sea surface directly ahead of steamers and fired crosswise in a shot interval of 50 m. The minimum offset (distance between shot and first receiver) is 150 m. Seismic data underwent standard pre-stack processing to optimize signal quality and imaging. Third-order seismic sequences Combining seismic interpretation with planktonic foraminifera and nannoplankton biostratigraphy, Qin (1996) determined second and third-order relative sea-level change across the study area (Fig. 2). Correlated by wireline logs and cores, Pang et al. (2006) divided the strata 23.8–8.2 Ma into eight sequences (Zhou et al., 2015). The se- quences, according to their basal boundaries SB23.8–SB10.5, are called SQ23.8, SQ21, SQ17.5 and SQ16.5 (Zhujiang Formation); SQ15.5, SQ13.8 and SQ12.5 (Hanjiang Formation); and SQ10.5 (Lower Yuehai Formation) (Fig. 2). The eight sequence boundaries are mainly marked by truncation and toplap terminations of seismic reflectors (Wang et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2015), and within the lowstand systems tracts in response to third-order globe sea-level falls (Fig. 2), and all sandy in lithology (Chen et al., 2015; He et al., 2020). Well data has now confirmed that sandy sequences formed in sequences SQ23.8 and SQ21, which are the main hydrocarbon-bearing intervals (Wang et al., 2012; Shi et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2015), while muddy sequences formed in sequences SQ17.5–SQ10.5 (Zhou et al., 2015), which correspond to an overall rapid rise of 2nd-order relative sea level (Fig. 2). 3.3. Seismic attributes Seismic attribute maps extracted from 3D seismic volume often reveal many notable characteristics that are not readily apparent from seismic cross − sections or temporal structural maps (Bull et al., 2009; Hart, 1999). To delineate MTD geometry and internal character, mul- tiple seismic attributes were extracted from the 3D volume. Coherence facilitated mapping discontinuities (faults, stratigraphic edges) and identifying chaotic facies associated with deformation (Deckers, 2015). Chaotic sections on coherence maps are seen as low coherence marked usually by dark, mottled patterns (Alves, 2015; Gamboa and Alves, 2015; Hart, 1999). Chaotic and disrupted seismic facies are easily discriminated from these maps (Bull et al., 2009). Seismic attribute of Root-Mean-Square (RMS) aided in recognizing the lateral boundary of sand and mud, leveraging the typically higher amplitude response of sands versus muds. (Bull et al., 2009; Luan and Lunt, 2022). Variance and dip attributes provided additional constraints on structural complexity and paleo-slope gradients within the deformed intervals, crucial for characterizing the MTD morphology and transport direction (Bull et al., 2009; Luan and Lunt, 2022; Banerjee et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2021). 3.4. Regional seismic profile interpretation The area of SCS experienced the Mesozoic volcanic arc or volcanic forearc (Li et al., 2018) to the Cenozoic rifting that resulted in the lithospheric breakup and finally seafloor spreading (Taylor and Hayes, 1983; Sun et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). Discussion 5.1. MTD in a confined environment For the past decades, continental margins provide an ideal setting for the mass transport deposits (MTDs). MTDs represent the failure, dislodgement, and downslope movement and deposition of sediment under the influence of gravity (Shanmugam et al., 1994; Shanmugam, 2020). After failure, the sediment moves downslope under gravity exhibiting extreme variability in mechanics (Shanmugam, 2020), ranging from solid blocks of kilometre-size coherence translation with or without rotation (Varnes, 1978) to transportation of small-size particles in debris flows or in turbidity flows (Cruden, 2003). According to Shanmugam’s (2020) recent classification, MTDs consist of three basic types: (1) slides, characterized by coherent translation without rotation; (2) slumps, involving coherent translation with rotation; and (3) debris flow. Notably, turbidity flows are excluded from this classification (Shanmugam, 2020; Dott, 1963). Typically, MTDs exhibit a headwall area featuring

Trigger mechanism MTDs generate primarily due to three factors: reduced support at the toe of the continental slope, excessive loading, and elevated pore-water pressure. They are generally governed by gravitational instability. Several factors have been proposed as potential triggers for seafloor gravitational instabilities, including high sedimentation rate (Hampton et al., 1996; Kuhlmann et al., 2017; Leynaud et al., 2007), sea level fluctuations (Smith et al., 2013; Urlaub et al., 2013), gas hydrate dissociation (Lee, 2009; Maslin et al., 2004), and steeper slope gradients (Owen et al., 2007; Urlaub et al., 2015). The steep slope gradients over 10˚have been reported for the landslide scarps area, and about 2˚for the slope gradients next to the headwall area in the northern continental margin of the SCS (Yang et al., 2024). For most of the MDTs in the sub- arctic mid-Norwegian margin, for almost its entire length, a gradient reaching 1˚is believed as the threshold value to trigger the gravity instability (Jing et al., 2022; Canals et al., 2004; He and Zhong, 2015). The Kaiping sag exhibits a gentle slope gradient of ~ 0.3–0.6◦, as evi- denced by seismic profiles (Fig. 10). This gradient is significantly lower than the quantitative thresholds for static slope stability (5-10◦, Cruden, 2003), and also falls below the threshold value reported for the sub- arctic mid-Norwegian margin (1˚, Jing et al., 2022; Canals et al., 2004; He and Zhong, 2015). The low slope gradient (~0.3–0.6◦) in the Kaiping sag directly contradicts the prevailing understanding that MTDs form via gravity-driven sliding on steep slopes (Shanmugam, 2012, 2020, 2021). Instead, our study proposes a hypothesis that dynamic triggers, such as mega-earthquakes, induce the chaotic facies

The low slope gradient (~0.3–0.6◦) in the Kaiping sag directly contradicts the prevailing understanding that MTDs form via gravity-driven sliding on steep slopes (Shanmugam, 2012, 2020, 2021). Instead, our study proposes a hypothesis that dynamic triggers, such as mega-earthquakes, induce the chaotic facies and the subsequent limited material migration. Mega-earthquakes generate dynamic shocks that liquefy unconsolidated sediments, enabling chaotic facies genera- tion regardless of whether slopes are gentle or steep. Gravity-driven mechanisms rely primarily on slope angle, while our hypothesis high- light the crucial role of earthquake shock in the formation of the chaotic facies within confined environments. Earthquakes are recognized as the primary cause of liquefaction in submarine unconsolidated layers (Du et al., 2025). Earthquake induced liquefaction was first reported in 1960 s (Ambraseys and Sarma, 1969; Seed, 1979; Sims, 1973, 1975). Hance (2003) reported a database of 534 submarine mass liquefaction events with over 40 % attributed to earthquakes. In the Mediterranean Sea, earthquakes account for nearly half of the 696 recorded submarine liquefaction (Urgeles and Camer- lenghi, 2013). Liquefaction is associated with the increasing in pore pressure generated by earthquake shocks and the rising of buried low- density materials. Phenomena relate to liquefaction after earthquakes including geyser-like fountains, mud volcanoes and sediment eruption (Obermeier, 1996). Only sufficiently large earthquakes can induce liquefaction and fluidization effects (Owen and Moretti, 2008). Liquefaction is unlikely in earthquake below magnitude 5, while stronger quakes may cause it over a broader area (Allen, 1986; Ambraseys, 1988; Galli, 2000). Deeper layer lique- faction by subsequent earthquake shock has not been reported, it is important to note that for the liquefaction of these deeply buried layers to occur, they must remain unconsolidated (Davenport et al., 1994; Owen and Moretti, 2008). Unconsolidated sediments, primarily siliceous ooze and carbon- ate deposits, extended to depths of ~ 600 m due to high sedimentation rates (~8 cm/kyr) following seafloor spreading (~16 Ma, T40 in this study). Sun et al. (2018) reported that four Quaternary submarine landslides along the northern margin of SCS all occurred during the high sea level period. These events are interpreted to be favored by high sea levels, elevated pore-water content, and a lower sand-mud ratio, which promote me- chanical sliding. Seismic data and ODP/IODP records from the northern SCS slope reveal extensive Quaternary landslides (~1,035 km3), pri- marily influenced by tectonic overpressure (earthquake-triggered) rather than deltaic collapse (gravity driven). The determined ages of the four landslides- ~1.59 Ma, 0.79 Ma, 0.54 Ma and 0.19 Ma −all fall within high sea level periods (Sun et al., 2018) (Fig. 12). Under elevated stress conditions (e.g. ultra-high pore-pressure or earthquake triggering), submarine unconsolidated layer undergo liquefaction (Du et al., 2025; Hance, 2003; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013). The literature identifies key indicators of large-scale liquefaction on continental margins, including pockmarks, mud diapirs, and tectonic uplift (Alves, 2010; Canals et al., 2004; Gee et al., 2007; Lamarche et al., 2008) (Fig. 11). Such large-scale liquefaction significantly enhances the mobility of submarine unconsolidated layers, leading to their subse- quent fluidization (Deckers, 2015; Martínez et al., 2005; Gong et al., 2014; Strasser et al., 2011). Pockmarks initiated development in the stratum above T31, progressively migrating upward toward structural highs and concentrating to form pockmark groups between T31 and T30 (Figs. 13, 14). The mge-3 earthquake generated the formation of the diapir struc- tures at T10 time (diagnosed by the onlapping at T10, as illustrated in Fig. 14). This event likely caused liquefaction of previously deposited strata, facilitating upward fluid migration via diapiric structures and associated thrust faults. The pockmark cluster observed between T31 and T30 (Fig. 14), concentrated on the structure high (Fig. 13b), and the gas zones accumulated at T10 (Fig. 13a) can be reasonably attributed to the combined effects of liquefaction by mega-earthquake, and subse- quent fluid migration by folding and uplift, and compressional thrusting (Du et al., 2025; Hance, 2003; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013). The development of the diapiric structure may provide evidence that the entire stratum may remain in an unconsolidated state (Fig. 11). The diapiric structures penetrate the entire sediment column and extend to the seafloor, indicating that the fluid activity through these structures may persist to the present day following their formation (Figs. 13, 14). 6.
